summary: Julia Evans compiled a crowd-sourced list of modern command line tools after asking on Twitter about newer alternatives to standard Unix utilities. The list covers direct replacements for classic tools like grep, find, ls, and du, as well as entirely new inventions that solve problems the original Unix toolkit didn't address. She organizes them into categories: replacements for standard tools, new inventions, JSON/YAML/CSV tools, grep variants, and lesser-known older utilities. The post serves as a practical reference for anyone looking to modernize their terminal workflow, and she closes by sharing her personal favorites and the tools she's most excited to try.
key_points: Many modern CLI tools are direct replacements for classic Unix utilities, often written in Rust, offering better defaults, speed, and UX (ripgrep, fd, bat, exa, dust)

A second category of tools represents genuinely new ideas that didn't exist in the traditional Unix toolkit, like fzf for fuzzy finding, entr for file watching, and croc for peer-to-peer file transfer
The JSON/YAML/CSV ecosystem has grown significantly, with jq spawning a family of similar tools for different formats (yq, fq, htmlq, xsv)
Some valuable older tools like pv, vidir, and sponge from moreutils remain underappreciated despite being widely useful
The list was crowd-sourced from Twitter, demonstrating how community knowledge-sharing surfaces tools people might otherwise never discover
money_quotes: My favourites of these that I use already are entr, ripgrep, git-delta, httpie, plocate, and jq.
I think the most exciting tool I learned about is vidir.
pbcopy/pbpaste maybe aren't "new" but were mentioned a lot.
key_insight: The Unix command line is experiencing a renaissance of modern replacements and novel tools that improve on decades-old defaults, and crowd-sourcing recommendations is an effective way to discover them.
